sentences.text: Not a family member?
In what time?
In Russia?
Any-- yeah, after you left your home.
After I left the home.
Yeah, we got some-- some Christians came out and they brought us some food.
In Losice, in the forest.
In the nearby-- no.
In the nearby villages.
Just our family.
It was only one woman who came out.
The older woman she brought out some food.
That's it.
No.
Yeah.
Stalingrad.
For the labor camp, for the government.
A labor camp.
Hard labor.
Yeah.
They-- they took and they put in in that camp, you know, criminals and refugees-- from-- from other countries.
No.
Only men.
Were they only Jews or were they Christians, too?
All kinds.
Poland.
And different kind of countries.
Even from-- from Russia.
You know, criminals.
Oh, yeah.
There were different countries, but I don't know exactly the names of the countries.
But it's all kind of different savages.
From different-- from different countries.
Yeah, it was-- yeah, it was gypsies.
It was all kinds.
But the thing is, before they-- they took us in that-- in that labor camp, I worked in a-- heard about the Rostov at the big city.
We worked in Rostov in a shop, in a government shop.
Rostov.
Rostov.
And we-- we got in there in that shop.
And we worked in that shop.
And all of  a sudden, about a couple of months after they liquidated the shop.
And they-- they took us in.
I used to make there for big officers, you know, boots and shoes for their girlfriends.
Big officers.
So then, all of a sudden, I'm in a very big group, and they send us away to Stalingrad.
And we went through the Volga-- it was frozen.
We went through, walked through the Volga in the coldness.
It's Siberia area.
And then we walked--first we walked to [NON-ENGLISH], then we walked through the Volga, Volgograd.
All walking in the deepest snow.
And we came to Stalingrad.
And we made a long road for the train-- to build a road for the train, to still run the Germans behind, eight miles behind.
And there the main leader from that group was a commissar.
And that commissar-- I made for him, stuff in that shop, I made for him, goods.
And he recognized me.
And he said to me, how come you're here?
I said, well, they threw me in here.
He said, I'm going to do something for you.
OK.
So it was very cold, windy.
It was coming out a wet snow.
And half an hour, and hour after wet snow freeze.
My clothes was frozen like that.
And I-- I saw him, and I talked to him.
I said, it's impossible for me to pick up a piece of wood for they have to make the road in that snow, in that frozen, frozen-- frozen in the ground.
He said to me, you have to hide yourself under the bushes flying around on the fields.
And when it's going to get dark, you come in in that bunker.
We hide him in the bunker.
So I came in in the bunker.
And he said to me, I'll do something for you in the morning.
So in the morning, he said, come to the bunker.
Because of me, he took in the morning-- he took me, and another two barbers, two tailors, and two shoemakers, me and my little brother.
And took us, and they gave us a special bunker.
We were [INAUDIBLE] by five or six Germans, dead.
And he-- I'm sorry.
Could you say that again?
Five, six Germans dead in the bunker.
It was the whole field was dead Germans.
The other side, it was Russians dead.
It was-- was the big war is just almost finished there.
And we have to make the road to circle around, around eight miles further.
So when he told us, gave us tools, to take out the dead Germans and make that bunker bigger.
And we're going to do that.
We're going to work there.
So we made-- we made out table from the ground, we made a bench.
And I [LAUGHS] was fixing the shoes for thousands of people.
And-- and the tailors-- all of them.
And we used to go around in that bunker.
It was so-- it was so freezing.
We'd have to--with hammers-- the-- the-- the knocking in the ground, to make a little bit wider, bigger the place.
Yeah.
The soldiers who was hiding there and shooting.
Yeah.
It was dirt.
It was the ground.
So in the meantime, we were so cold, and it was hard to work in that cold weather.
I was walking around forth and back and knocking my foot in a foot, not to get freezing up my toes.
And my brother and the other guys, who was mad at me, they said they're going to tell the commissar.
I said, you tell him.
I'm not afraid.
And I didn't work that hard because I know I'm going to lose my own foot.
My brother got frozen up.
They took them out the bones from his toes.
So we-- we build up that bunker.
They gave us some kerosene to keep it light inside.
It was working.
It was-- then every time I stick out my-- we-- we covered up the bunker with the-- with the bushes flying around.
We-- you put it in-- we put it in, and that bunker was a lot of bunkers, for the one German with his hands up on the top, so we notice this bunker.
It was-- the body of a German?
Yeah.
Yeah, he was frozen.
Yeah, he must have freezed to death.
And then we-- every few minutes, you will see another German, another officer walking around, half frozen, and asking for the office to get him in there, the office.
It was a lot of Germans still alive there.
[DRINKS]
So anyway, we was there until we started to get it warmer.
It was getting spring, you know.
It was a very nice couple of months.
We're building the whole thing.
May I interrupt to just ask a question?
When you saw the Germans and they saw you, what transpired?
Well, he-- he asked me for the office.
And nobody couldn't talk German there.
And I-- I took a couple of them.
A few times, I took them to the office, to his office.
It was-- it was in our tent.
And I-- and I-- I to them.
Some-- one speaked German so hard to understand.
Because in Germany, this all depends what area they speak the difference.
But I still could-- could tell them what are you saying.
And they got [NON-ENGLISH], they got a nice Jewish guy, a rabbi's son.
But he wasn't there at that time.
And they have to talk to the officer.
So I have to translate it.
Well, they-- they want-- they want help, because they was frozen there.
They want food.
They were in a strange land.
So, and I went with him to the office and I explained what he was saying to the-- to the Russians, to the Russian officers.
And then it was there-- I was there until it started to get warmer.
And the mines started to go off.
And the [INAUDIBLE].
So I wasn't there already.
I was trying to talk to the officer to send us-- to get us out from there.
So they-- he said to me that the commissar is going to send us to in the back in Russia.
In Russia, there were [INAUDIBLE] there, to make him-- to make him nice shoes.
So I was-- my feet was swollen like that.
Swollen and hunger.
You know, my brother got a problem with his foot, they have to put them out.
I have to carry him on my back.
So we went-- he said, I can't send you out from here until you get it from a doctor, a paper.
You can do nothing anymore.
You are sick.
So, and the doctor was a good friend of ours-- a Jewish guy.
He was not even a doctor.
He was-- he was running the SS-- the SS-- a bath, you know?
I'm sorry?
They're taking baths, you know, showers.
He was in charge of the place--
He was in charge for the doctor.
But he was not a doctor, still, but he was in charge.
Anyway, I went to him and I told him, we have to get out of here because we are frozen and swollen.
So he said, well, he wants something from us.
Because I got a box with leather, with all kinds of things.
So I gave it and he gave us a paper.
And the commissar hired from the-- from the-- from village, not far, a sled, with four horses.
And they took us out to the nearest station.
And we went the way deep in Russia-- so we went in the area in here about Saratov.
Hear about Saratov?
Big city, Saratov.
And not far from Saratov was a little-- a little small city.
And we opened up a shop.
We didn't went there-- we came there.
We open up a shop.
And here, I got a picture in the shop, where I was teaching-- I was teaching kids, you know, the criminals, to make shoes.
Yeah.
[LAUGHS]
A town in our own, in private.
No.
In private.
And they gave us-- they took us out, boys, you know, bad boys, in-- in the jails, and they want us to teach them to make shoes.
Well, this was in '43.
No.
We knew they are in big, big danger.
But then in that year, in-- the '43, they took us, they called us, you know, to the army many times.
Not my brother, because he couldn't walk.
So me.
Every six weeks, I have to go to the army or to a-- or to the doctor's to check.
So finally, the doctors-- it was two doctors who was from the city, and I made them shoes.
And they--they tried to get me out of it, and they don't let me get-- they want to take us in the Russian army.
So anyway, end of the-- end of the-- it was 1944.
And that commissar in the-- in the army, he said to us, you have to go now.
We already organized the Polish army.
And they already-- they are in Poland.
And now you're going to go to the Russian army.
So they sent us to the Russian army.
And we came in this in Saratov.
In-- there was not in Saratov a city.
And they said, this is Saratov.
This is the main-- big, big city.
And then they formed the army.
And they took us in under the tents.
Was this the Russian army or the Polish?
In the Russian.
No.
They take us in the Russian army.
And we came-- and we didn't speak Russian.
We just speak Polish.
We said, we're not-- we're not Russians.
We want the Polish army.
He said, what do you want?
The Polish army is in Poland, already.
Not there.
So anyway, we tried hard to get out from them.
And finally, they gave us a paper to go in the Polish army.
The Polish army was in Lublin already, you know.
So they gave us a paper, and we went-- [DRINKS] on our own.
Me and my younger brother, we came to Kostopol back.
We stopped in-- we have to go straight to Lublin.
But we didn't go, we stopped in Kostopol.
We stopped in Kostopol.
We wanted to find out what happened to our family if they was there.
We was looking around.
We didn't see no Jews.
No one.
No Jews.
So we came-- we find-- finally, we find the guy, one Polack that told us, one guy is alive.
And I knew that guy.
I worked with him before in-- in that little city.
And he knew that somebody well that was-- was taking care of him, and they came out alive.
And he saw the whole thing what's going on there when the German came.
The Germans.
No.
When I went away.
When I came back, it was the Russians.
It belongs to the Russians.
His name?
I don't know the second name.
I know one name.
The first name it was Fischel.
Fischel, his name.
He was with a long beard.
He was a  young man with a long beard.
He was hiding between the Christians there.
So someone was protecting him.
Protecting, yeah.
They protect him.
Well, finally we find him.
And finally we talk to him.
And he knew my parents.
He knew me.
I was with him working in a shop.
Ukrainians and friends-- his friends.
He lived there, he was born there.
No.
So anyway, he said, if you are staying, they announced the Germans, all the men to come in the-- in the playground.
We are going to send them away in a safe place.
So my father was from the first, all the men.
They make him to take off their clothes.
Put it away in the side.
And they make him to dig a big trough all the way.
And they make him to stand up naked by the grave and they shoot him in.
Did this Mr.
Fischel see this?
Yeah.
Yeah, he saw it because he was-- he was hiding.
He was between the-- Ukrainians.
Then they-- they shoot them, all the men.
It was not a big city with Jewish people.
It was-- it was a nice couple thousand.
So anyway, they shoot all the men.
And then a couple days later, they called-- they put it in papers.
Announced, the wives and the children of the men to come in that-- in that playground.
They're going to send them to their mens, their husbands.
It was so tricky.
And they came.
My mother, my sisters.
Then they make them to dig a separate grave.
It was-- it was three graves there.
And they shoot them in.
The same thing.
Then they went, they brought from Rowne-- Rowne is a big city.
And they bring from Rowne 3,000 Jews.
And they buried them.
They made the same thing in that-- in that playground.
When we came, and we heard that story, we went to that-- to see the graves.
And that Fischel told me the graves it was bleeding for all year.
Three graves.
It was there, buried about 4,000 Jews-- men, women, and children.
You know, I was close to the grave.
It was a forest, a little forest there.
And it was an organization, AK, in Poland.
The organization it was-- it was-- a General Komorowski, his name.
He was a general in that organization of Polacks.
And they-- any Jew they find out, they killed them.
Even not by Hitler, by the Germans.
I got a book.
I got a book.
No.
No.
I was close to the grave.
The AK, the organization that was shooting out from there, and I can't get close.
Now that's on the Russian side.
I couldn't see it.
It was dangerous to go close.
And at that time, we was hanging around there, we was trying to go closer and closer.
Even when they were shooting out from that-- that forest.
Yeah.
It was shooting at us.
Yeah.
Because they didn't want nobody to come close to the graves.
They was not better than the Germans.
It was the organization, the AK-- they-- they destroyed a lot of Jews after the war.
Well, this is-- AK stands-- the-- the -- the-- Krajowa Army.
The army for the land, for the Polish land.
Armia Krajowa.
Under the leadership from General Komorowski.
Mr.
Fischel.
And the organization, they always had memorial time.
In that time, I marked it there in Hebrew in [NON-ENGLISH]-- 12 days in hell.
This is-- this-- in this-- I have to check in the Jewish calendar there, what is the-- the month.
No, then we went-- they-- the-- the Russian arrest us, and they said they're going to send us in the Russian army.
I said, I got a paper for the Polish army.
So we went to Lublin by train, ourselves.
And we came there and we went in the Polish army.
We got in at [NON-ENGLISH], this is in Polish.
[NON-ENGLISH], and that group, we went to the farmers collecting food for the army to go on the front line army.
Uniform-- sure.
First, we got in-- my younger brother wants to go to what he thinks to go.
And I don't want to go there, because I know this is suicide right away.
So I told him to go, when I go, to stay next to me.
So finally, we see they picked out all the people, not young.
So we-- we have-- first, we want to shave.
We didn't shave.
We got a little beard, so we look a little bit older.
So when I see speaking only these guys, and they came to me, and he asked me how I feel-- if I'm strong enough.
I said, yes.
So they took us both in that group.
And when they-- and then-- in an hour later, they took us in a big-- big magazine.
You know, this-- in a big place.
And they put us in a little new, brand new Polish clothes.
And they gave us grenades with machine guns.
My brother said to me, here, you pick the right thing.
You're going to get killed-- in a day or two.
So finally, he was wrong and I was right.
So in that army-- first of all, I got a machine gun.
I never used it.
The big officer, he got a big family, and he needs shoes.
It's no shoes, no shoes nowhere.
So he needs me.
And I made him shoes.
And-- and I worked all these-- two years in the army, and I worked.
Because they-- they need shoes.
The big shots, the big officers.
And then my office-- they called me in, in the office.
I got a shop-- a shop in the-- in the city.
The name, it was [NON-ENGLISH], in that city.
I got a shop, with machinery, and I made the officer boots.
And they got-- they got materials.
And once the officer-- my officer said to me, I'm going to have a general, a Russian general, and I-- you have to make him a pair of boots.
So I made him a pair of boots.
And the second day, I brought in the boots.
And he put it in, and he was very, very satisfied.
He said, for these boots, you put a man for a sergeant.
Want to make me for a sergeant.
So I said, I don't want that title-- I can get along without it.
Because if I take the thing, I will have to stay another three, four months in the army.
So anyway, I guess it was in that city, in [NON-ENGLISH], they announced the war is over.
So the first time I walked out from the shop, I put it in the machine here, and I went to the city--it was a beautiful city.
And I shoot in the air for the first time.
Is that the only time you used the gun?
I got a chance to shoot.
When the war was over--
When the war was over, we went-- we find out that all the Jewish people is going to Lodz.
You heard about Lodz?
And none of us there the Jewish federation.
It was most-- all the Jews who was going to Lodz.
We came to Lodz.
We talked to a lot of people.
We met people from our own city, or they came from Russia, from all over.
And we saw them, talked to them.
And they said, the best places to go is to Waldenburg, the big German city.
We came to Waldenburg.
It was already many, many Jews.
And they took over all the kind of places, businesses.
And it was a-- was a Russian Jewish guy became the mayor in the city.
And we went to him, and we said, we run a shop.
They took away from the Germans shops, stores, and they gave it to the Jewish guys.
So when we was in army clothes, and we came in there.
And we said, we run a shop.
Finally we got a shop.
And we was making shoes there.
So now to go and get leather material for shoes, you couldn't get it to Waldenburg even though it was a big city.
So I-- [DRINKS] in the mean time, came to me a guy.
And he said to me, you know you got a sister?
He said, she was in Auschwitz.
And she is in Sosnowiec, IN Katowice-- she was in Katowice.
And I called her up.
I find out right away how to get in touch with her.
And she was-- she left.
She was with an older man.
She was going with a half a million to Lodz by bringing [NON-ENGLISH] for the city.
Katowice, it was a very big city.
And she was going with millions, with half a millions.
When she find out, she dropped everything.
She came to me.
Money.
Half a million money.
Twice to Lodz buy an [NON-ENGLISH] for Katowice.
In-- in zlotys.
You know, Polish money.
So anyway, she came to me.
She dropped it.
Then I said, well, you have to go back with your partner.
You to get some money, you have to get married.
She's got before husband and a child.
They destroyed them.
So anyway, she went back, but she didn't go-- she went to him, and he said-- she went to him, she saw him.
And he didn't ask her for-- on the brothers.
She got mad and she left there.
So then she came back to Waldenburg.
And I said, what did you do?
You have to get some money from him.
You were his partner.
So anyway, she asked me I got a girl, or if I got married or what.
I said, no.
I can't find nobody after the war.
She said, I got a girl for you.
[LAUGHS] So after which she gave me a letter to see my wife.
And my wife got with her sister a supermarket in Katowice, in the same city.
And I went there.
So anyway, I took money.
I got a-- my sister told me it's a lot of letter there in Katowice.
So I started to get some money from my friends for my work.
I got about six shoemakers already.
I got a shop.
So I take all the money what I can get it.
And I went there, and I saw my wife.
They have to change.
And what was her name?
And then we're going to stop for a tape change.
What was this young woman's name?
My wife?
[NON-ENGLISH].
Last name was Koenigsburg.
